# GTM Revenue Operations Showcase Business Spec

## Purpose

This document is the umbrella business specification for the full GTM ANIP
showcase.

It replaces the narrower Phase 1 framing of the earlier `business-spec.md`
document and is intended to cover the complete GTM system as it exists now:

- pipeline analytics
- enrichment
- prioritization
- outreach drafting
- actor-aware permission boundaries
- approval-gated operational previews
- auditability
- governed multi-service composition

This is the business-facing source artifact that Studio should translate into:

1. PM-readable requirements and scenarios
2. developer design and service boundaries
3. generated code and extension points
4. validation of the running services against intended behavior
5. regression-backed proof that the implementation still matches the design

## Problem

Revenue operations teams increasingly want agent experiences that can:

- answer pipeline and forecast questions quickly
- identify risk and bottlenecks
- enrich account context
- prioritize work
- draft outreach
- prepare operational actions

The default market approach is usually a prompt-heavy agent with broad CRM or
warehouse access. That is hard to review, hard to govern, and difficult to
trust once the workflow becomes multi-step or actor-sensitive.

The showcase exists to prove a different pattern:

- business intent is defined up front
- bounded ANIP capabilities are designed explicitly
- the implementation is generated and completed from that design
- the running services are validated against the intended design
- agent runtimes consume governed services instead of raw tools

## Business Goal

Build a GTM agent system that lets revenue users and GTM operators:

- inspect pipeline health, forecast posture, risk, and bottlenecks
- review team and product performance
- enrich account context and identify lookalikes
- score and prioritize inbound or at-risk work
- draft bounded outreach content
- prepare follow-up and reassignment plans

while preserving:

- bounded service behavior
- actor-aware visibility and masking
- approval-gated operational actions
- explicit clarification instead of guessing
- explicit denial or restriction instead of overreach
- auditable behavior across single-service and multi-service flows

## System Scope

The GTM showcase is a multi-service system composed of four bounded ANIP
services.

The following capability inventory is canonical for this showcase. Studio must
preserve these exact capability IDs in Developer Design, package generation, and
showcase verification. Do not introduce paraphrased replacement IDs for these
business behaviors.

### 1. Pipeline Service

Service:

- `gtm-pipeline-service`

Capabilities:

- `gtm.pipeline_summary`
- `gtm.pipeline_forecast_summary`
- `gtm.stage_bottleneck_summary`
- `gtm.sales_team_performance_summary`
- `gtm.product_pipeline_summary`
- `gtm.stalled_opportunity_review`
- `gtm.account_risk_summary`
- `gtm.prepare_followup_tasks`
- `gtm.prepare_reassignment_plan`
- `gtm.at_risk_followup_preparation`
- `gtm.at_risk_reassignment_preparation`

This service owns the governed CRM-state layer.

### 2. Enrichment Service

Service:

- `gtm-enrichment-service`

Capabilities:

- `gtm.account_enrichment_summary`
- `gtm.lookalike_accounts`
- `gtm.at_risk_account_enrichment_summary`

This service owns bounded external-style context enrichment over the approved
account scope.

### 3. Prioritization Service

Service:

- `gtm-prioritization-service`

Capabilities:

- `gtm.score_leads`
- `gtm.prioritize_accounts`
- `gtm.route_leads`
- `gtm.prioritized_routing_preparation`

This service owns deterministic scoring, ranking, and approval-gated routing.

### 4. Outreach Service

Service:

- `gtm-outreach-service`

Capabilities:

- `gtm.draft_outreach_message`
- `gtm.suggest_followup_content`
- `gtm.objection_response_variants`
- `gtm.prioritized_outreach_draft`
- `gtm.bottleneck_account_outreach_draft`

This service owns bounded draft-only outreach behavior. It does not send
messages.

## Cross-Service Capability Semantics

Some showcase capabilities are intentionally cross-service from a business
perspective, but still have one canonical owning service in the ANIP contract.
They are not hidden agent recipes and they are not generic consumer-side
workflow glue.

- `gtm.at_risk_account_enrichment_summary` is owned by
  `gtm-enrichment-service`. It enriches accounts selected from a bounded
  at-risk account review without exposing raw enrichment data.
- `gtm.at_risk_followup_preparation` is owned by `gtm-pipeline-service`.
  It composes a bounded at-risk account review into follow-up task preparation
  and stops at `approval_required` before any task is created.
- `gtm.at_risk_reassignment_preparation` is owned by `gtm-pipeline-service`.
  It composes a bounded at-risk or overloaded pipeline review into a
  reassignment preview and stops before ownership changes are executed.
- `gtm.prioritized_routing_preparation` is owned by
  `gtm-prioritization-service`. It composes lead scoring/prioritization into a
  routing preview and stops at `approval_required` before downstream routing.
- `gtm.prioritized_outreach_draft` is owned by `gtm-outreach-service`. It
  drafts outreach for an explicitly selected prioritized account or cohort and
  stops before any send behavior.
- `gtm.bottleneck_account_outreach_draft` is owned by `gtm-outreach-service`.
  It drafts outreach for either an explicitly selected account from a
  bottleneck/risk review or a provider-selected bounded top candidate from that
  review, then stops at approval or preview. The service owns this derived
  target selection boundary; the consuming agent must not guess the account in
  app glue.

The consuming agent or app may orchestrate calls across services, but the
public capability IDs above remain service-owned contract surface. Any generated
implementation must preserve these IDs so the regression bank can compare the
same governed behavior across Python, TypeScript, Go, Java, and C#.

## Primary Users And Actors

The showcase must support different governed outcomes for different actors.

Representative actor families:

- `sales_leader`
- `rev_ops_manager`
- `account_manager_east`
- `account_manager_west`
- `sales_analyst`

The business expectation is not simply endpoint access control. The expectation
is that actor identity can change:

- what data is visible
- what fields are masked
- what scope is allowed
- what actions can be prepared
- what actions require approval
- what actions are denied

## Core Business Questions

The system must support recurring GTM questions such as:

- What does the current quarter pipeline look like by stage or region?
- What is the risk-adjusted forecast for a quarter or region?
- Which accounts are most at risk, and why?
- Where are deals bottlenecking?
- Which teams or managers are underperforming?
- How is pipeline distributed by product line?
- What enrichment context explains why these accounts matter?
- Which accounts or leads should be prioritized next?
- What follow-up or reassignment work should be prepared?
- What first-touch or follow-up outreach should be drafted for an approved
  target?

These questions must be supported through bounded capabilities, not through a
generic “query anything in CRM” surface.

## Capability Expectations

Every business capability in this system must satisfy the following conditions:

1. it answers a recurring business question
2. it uses a stable parameter set
3. it has a clear governed outcome
4. it preserves explicit service ownership
5. it is reviewable in Studio and testable in the regression harness

The system should support BI-shaped reads where appropriate, but only through
declared semantic capabilities and allowlisted dimensions, filters, and
measures. It must not expose raw SQL or arbitrary database exploration through
the agent layer.

## Required Governed Behaviors

The system must make these behaviors explicit and reviewable:

### Success

- in-scope bounded read or draft behavior returns a governed result

### Clarification

- the system does not guess missing critical fields such as quarter, account
  reference, ranking basis, or outreach target

### Restriction

- the system may narrow or mask the result when an actor is partially allowed
  to see a bounded slice but not the full requested scope

### Denial

- the system denies raw export, direct-send, or out-of-scope requests instead
  of improvising unsafe behavior

### Approval Required

- the system may prepare operational previews but must stop before routing,
  reassignment, follow-up execution, or any other write-adjacent side effect

## Approval Posture

The showcase must demonstrate that operational preparation and execution are
not the same thing.

Allowed preparation examples:

- follow-up task preview
- reassignment plan preview
- lead routing preview

Required behavior:

- preview can be generated when the actor is allowed to prepare it
- the system returns `approval_required` for the next step
- the approval record is durable and reviewable
- a lower-authority actor can be denied even when the same question is allowed
  for a higher-authority actor

## Auditability Requirements

Every governed call must be reconstructable after the fact.

The system must be able to explain:

- who asked
- which actor identity or role applied
- which capability ran
- which parameters were used after normalization
- whether the outcome was success, clarification, restriction, denial, or
  approval-required
- why two different actors received different results

Audit is a core part of the business trust story, not a technical afterthought.

## Multi-Service Composition Requirements

The system must support compound user questions that require more than one
bounded service hop while preserving the same governed boundaries.

Representative composition families:

- `pipeline/risk -> enrichment`
- `prioritization -> enrichment -> outreach`
- `forecast -> risk -> follow-up preview`
- `score -> route`
- `bottleneck -> risk -> enrichment`

The required business property is:

- the question may be compound
- the execution remains governed

That means:

- service chaining is bounded
- approval stops still apply mid-chain
- denial still applies mid-chain
- clarification still applies at the correct step
- actor-aware boundaries still hold across the chain

## Data And Verification Posture

The showcase uses warehouse-modeled GTM data plus bounded auxiliary layers.

The business expectation is:

- the agent consumes governed ANIP capabilities
- the data layer is modeled and inspectable
- a BI verification surface can validate the same read-oriented business slices
- governance still remains with ANIP services, not the BI tool

The verification surface is allowed to validate:

- pipeline
- forecast
- bottlenecks
- risk
- team performance
- product pipeline
- enrichment slices

It is not expected to replicate:

- actor-aware denial or masking
- approval behavior
- compound orchestration
- outreach generation

## Validation Intent

The showcase is only successful if the full lifecycle is visible and repeatable:

1. business spec exists as a first-class project asset
2. Studio translates that spec into PM-readable requirements and scenarios
3. Studio derives developer design and service shape from those artifacts
4. generated code is actually used in the running implementation
5. explicit extension logic has a declared place and does not hide in
   overwritten generated files
6. Studio can observe and validate the running services against intended
   behavior and ANIP metadata
7. the regression harness passes across single-service and compound flows

## Non-Goals

This showcase is not trying to prove:

- a single unconstrained GTM super-agent
- raw SQL freedom for the agent
- unrestricted CRM export
- direct-send outreach
- write execution without approval
- one giant merged service instead of explicit boundaries
- hidden prompt logic acting as the policy engine

## Success Criteria

The showcase is successful when an informed reviewer can see that:

- the business intent was written first
- the PM and developer views derive from that business intent
- the implementation follows the bounded service design
- extensions are explicit instead of hidden inside regenerated files
- the same services can be consumed by multiple agent runtimes
- permissions, approvals, denial, clarification, and auditability survive
  compound workflows
- the full system can be reproduced locally and validated end to end

# GTM Showcase Business Spec

## Purpose

This is the canonical PM-readable business specification for the GTM ANIP
showcase.

It is intentionally not written as a Studio-internal artifact first.

This document defines expected behavior classes and representative requests,
not an exhaustive language inventory.

The showcase has to prove that a business document like this can be translated
into bounded behavior specifications, developer design, generated service code,
implementation validation, and real agent execution.

## Problem

Revenue and GTM teams need agents that can answer pipeline-health questions,
identify at-risk deals, and prepare follow-up work without exposing raw data or
improvising unsafe write behavior.

The current default pattern in the market is usually:

- a general-purpose agent
- prompts
- broad tool access to CRM or data systems
- weak reviewability when the agent narrows, guesses, or overreaches

That pattern is not good enough for serious in-house GTM operations.

## Business Goal

Build a bounded GTM agent experience that lets a revenue operator or GTM user:

- understand current pipeline health for a quarter or region
- identify the accounts and opportunities most at risk
- understand why those accounts are risky through bounded evidence
- prepare follow-up work
- stop cleanly at approval before any downstream mutation

## What The Agent Must Be Able To Do

For Phase 1, the agent must be able to:

- summarize bounded pipeline health
- identify stalled opportunities
- rank risky accounts with explicit evidence
- prepare follow-up tasks for review

For Phase 1, it must not attempt to:

- enrich accounts from outside datasets
- score new inbound leads
- draft outreach content
- execute follow-up mutations without approval
- export raw row-level CRM data

## Behavior Classes

The PM is not expected to enumerate every possible user utterance.

The PM is expected to define:

- bounded capability scope
- behavior families
- representative scenario families
- critical boundaries and edge cases

The relevant behavior classes for this showcase are:

1. clear in-scope bounded read
- return available results with bounded evidence

2. ambiguity requiring clarification
- do not guess missing quarter, ranking basis, or scope

3. broad or unsafe data requests
- in Phase 1, deny raw row-level exports rather than improvising a narrower interpretation

That is a Phase 1 policy choice for this showcase, not a universal ANIP rule.

4. operational write preparation
- allow plan preparation
- stop at `approval_required` before downstream side effects

5. out-of-scope requests
- deny rather than improvise

## Representative Scenario Requests

- “Which deals in our Q2 pipeline are at risk this quarter, and why?”
- “Show me all opportunities stuck longer than 30 days.”
- “Rank the highest-risk accounts in our Q2 pipeline.”
- “Prepare follow-up tasks for the highest-risk accounts in my Q2 pipeline.”
- “Show me raw row-level records for our Q2 pipeline.”

These are representative scenario requests, not an exhaustive list.

## Business Safety Posture

- the system must not guess missing critical parameters
- the system must not expose raw exports when the bounded capability is meant to
  return governed summaries
- the system must not execute downstream operational changes without approval
- the system must make its restriction, denial, clarification, and approval
  behavior visible and reviewable

## Validation Intent

This business spec is representative, not exhaustive.

The showcase is successful only if the following can be demonstrated:

- this business spec is visible as a source artifact
- Studio translates this spec into bounded requirements and scenarios
- Studio developer design derives an implementation shape from those
  requirements
- the running service code is generated from that design path, then completed
  and run
- Studio validates the running service against observed ANIP metadata and
  runtime behavior
- more than one agent runtime can consume the same governed service correctly

## Non-Goals

- proving a single unconstrained GTM super-agent
- covering every GTM workflow in the first release
- proving outbound enrichment, lead scoring, and outreach in Phase 1
- relying on hidden manual interpretation steps that Studio cannot represent
- expecting the PM to define every possible wording a user might use

entries.statement: Missing critical inputs trigger explicit clarification instead of guessing, including quarter, account scope, cohort, routing target, persona, or outreach target when required by the capability.

entries.evidence: Comparative actor review shows allowed, masked, restricted, or denied outcomes vary appropriately by actor identity and requested scope.
entries.statement: Actor-aware restriction and masking remain visible in runtime behavior so that different actors can receive different allowed scope, field visibility, or outcomes for the same business question.

entries.evidence: Preview scenarios return approval-gated outputs and do not execute writes; higher- and lower-authority actors show the expected differences in preparation versus denial.
entries.statement: Operational preparation flows stop cleanly at approval_required before any routing, reassignment, follow-up execution, or other downstream mutation.

entries.evidence: Cross-service scenarios show each step uses the canonical owning capability surface, with no hidden consumer-side policy substitution or merged super-service behavior.
entries.statement: Service ownership and capability boundaries remain explicit across the showcase, including compound flows that span more than one service.

entries.evidence: Representative multi-step flows demonstrate that governance survives service chaining rather than disappearing once orchestration begins.
entries.statement: Compound workflows preserve the same governed behaviors as single-service calls, including clarification, denial, restriction, masking, and approval stops at the correct step in the chain.

entries.evidence: Audit review for representative calls can explain both the final outcome and why different actors received different results.
entries.statement: Every governed call is auditable enough to reconstruct who asked, which actor identity applied, which capability ran, which normalized parameters were used, and why the outcome was success, clarification, restriction, denial, or approval-required.

entries.evidence: Local reproduction, Studio inspection, runtime metadata validation, and regression results all confirm the implementation still matches the intended bounded design.
entries.statement: The same governed services can be reproduced locally, inspected in Studio, and validated end to end through runtime checks and regression-backed review across supported flows.

entries.description: Bounded GTM pipeline review covering pipeline health, forecast posture, risk, bottlenecks, team performance, and product pipeline summaries for governed quarter- and scope-based analysis.

entries.description: Bounded enrichment of selected accounts with firmographic context, fit explanation, and lookalike discovery without exposing raw enrichment sources or mutating downstream systems.
entries.label: Account Enrichment

entries.description: Governed scoring, ranking, and routing recommendation for bounded lead or account cohorts, with explainable rationale and approval-gated downstream routing posture.
entries.label: Work Prioritization and Routing

entries.description: Draft-only GTM outreach preparation for selected leads or accounts, including first-touch, follow-up, and objection-response variants using explicit bounded context.
entries.label: Outreach Drafting

entries.description: Preparation of follow-up, reassignment, and routing previews that explain recommended actions while stopping at approval before any downstream execution.
entries.label: Operational Previews

entries.description: Governed visibility, restriction, masking, clarification, denial, and approval behavior that changes outcomes based on actor identity, allowed scope, and action sensitivity.
entries.label: Actor-Aware Governance

entries.description: Reviewable reconstruction and validation of governed service behavior, including who asked, which capability ran, normalized parameters, outcome type, and cross-service conformance to intended design.
entries.label: Auditability and Validation

entries.rationale: The umbrella and service specs repeatedly require bounded capabilities instead of unconstrained CRM, warehouse, BI, or query-language behavior.
entries.statement: This showcase is not a generic "query anything in CRM or the warehouse" agent surface.

entries.rationale: Multiple source specs deny raw exports across pipeline, enrichment, prioritization, and outreach surfaces.
entries.statement: This showcase does not expose raw row-level CRM, enrichment, scoring, transcript, or training-data exports through the agent experience.

entries.rationale: The source material consistently separates preparation from execution and requires approval stops before write-adjacent actions.
entries.statement: This showcase does not execute routing, reassignment, follow-up creation, outreach sending, or other downstream mutations without explicit approval.

entries.rationale: Actor-specific visibility differences are a core governed behavior across the pipeline and umbrella specs.
entries.statement: This showcase does not collapse actor-aware restriction, masking, and scope boundaries into one broad result for all users.

entries.rationale: The umbrella spec explicitly preserves bounded service ownership and rejects one giant merged service or hidden prompt logic as the policy engine.
entries.statement: This showcase is not one merged GTM super-service with hidden cross-service policy logic.

entries.rationale: The umbrella spec explicitly rejects hidden prompt logic acting as the policy engine and requires reviewable governed behavior.
entries.statement: This showcase does not rely on hidden prompt-only interpretation to decide governance, approvals, denials, or restrictions.

entries.rationale: The source specs repeatedly state that behavior is representative, bounded, and not an exhaustive language or workflow inventory.
entries.statement: This showcase is not trying to cover every GTM workflow or every possible user wording in the first release.

data.policy_summary: The GTM showcase should enforce actor-aware governed outcomes across analytics, enrichment, prioritization, outreach drafting, and operational previews. In-scope reads return bounded results, missing critical inputs trigger clarification, over-broad or unsafe requests are denied, partially allowed visibility may return restricted or masked results, and write-adjacent preparation stops at approval_required rather than executing downstream changes.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded preparation of follow-up, reassignment, or routing previews, but stop before any downstream execution or mutation.
rules.notes: Supported by the umbrella spec and preview-specific specs that distinguish preparation from execution.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded preparation of follow-up, reassignment, or routing previews, but stop before any downstream execution or mutation.
rules.notes: Applies to reassignment preview, routing preparation, and other write-adjacent preparation flows.

rules.governed_outcome: When a request exceeds the actor's allowed regional boundary, return a restricted result rather than exposing the broader scope.
rules.notes: The source repeatedly requires actor-aware restriction for region requests beyond allowed boundaries.

rules.governed_outcome: When a request exceeds the actor's allowed regional boundary, return a restricted result rather than exposing the broader scope.
rules.notes: The source repeatedly requires actor-aware restriction for region requests beyond allowed boundaries.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded analytic visibility where permitted, but mask financial values when the actor can see shape or trends without full numbers.
rules.notes: The forecast, bottleneck, team-performance, and product-pipeline specs all require actor-aware masking for some actors.

rules.governed_outcome: If a forecast, bottleneck, team-performance, or product-pipeline request is missing a critical quarter, ask for clarification instead of guessing.
rules.notes: Applies to bounded analytics reads where quarter is a required input.

rules.governed_outcome: If a forecast, bottleneck, team-performance, or product-pipeline request is missing a critical quarter, ask for clarification instead of guessing.
rules.notes: Applies to bounded analytics reads where quarter is a required input.

rules.governed_outcome: Deny raw bulk enrichment export, unsupported source exposure, outreach-only requests, lead-scoring requests, and downstream mutation requests in the enrichment service.
rules.notes: The enrichment service is bounded, read-only, and must not expose raw unconstrained enrichment exports.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded scoring, ranking, and routing recommendation preparation, but stop at approval before route execution or CRM mutation.
rules.notes: The prioritization service distinguishes recommendation from execution.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded scoring, ranking, and routing recommendation preparation, but stop at approval before route execution or CRM mutation.
rules.notes: The prioritization service distinguishes recommendation from execution.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded draft generation and variants for an explicit target and purpose, but do not send messages or expose raw transcript or training-corpus content.
rules.notes: The outreach service is draft-only in the first cut.

rules.governed_outcome: Allow bounded draft generation and variants for an explicit target and purpose, but do not send messages or expose raw transcript or training-corpus content.
rules.notes: The outreach service is draft-only in the first cut.

rules.governed_outcome: Governed calls must remain reconstructable with actor identity, capability used, normalized parameters, outcome type, and explanation for actor-specific differences.
rules.notes: This is a cross-cutting trust requirement rather than a user-facing business action.

data.approval_posture_summary: Read-oriented analytics, enrichment, and draft generation can return governed results directly when in scope. Operational next-step capabilities may prepare previews, but routing, reassignment, follow-up execution, and other write-adjacent actions must stop at approval_required, with authority-sensitive outcomes and durable reviewability.
business_goals: Let revenue users and GTM operators inspect pipeline health, forecast posture, risk, and bottlenecks through bounded capabilities.
Support review of team performance and product pipeline with explainable, actor-aware results.
Provide bounded account enrichment and lookalike analysis without unconstrained access to raw enrichment sources.
Score and prioritize leads or accounts with explainable rationale and safe routing recommendations.
Draft bounded outreach content without sending messages.
Prepare follow-up, routing, and reassignment previews while stopping at approval before downstream execution.
Preserve actor-aware visibility, masking, clarification, denial, approval gating, and auditability across single-service and multi-service flows.
data.business_problem: Revenue operations teams need agent experiences that can answer recurring GTM questions and prepare next steps, but prompt-heavy agents with broad CRM or warehouse access are hard to review, govern, and trust, especially across multi-step and actor-sensitive workflows.
data.governed_behavior_summary: The product must answer recurring GTM questions through bounded, explainable capabilities with stable parameters and clear service ownership. It must clarify missing critical inputs instead of guessing, restrict or mask results when actor scope is limited, deny raw export or other out-of-scope requests, remain read-only or draft-only where specified, and preserve the same governed boundaries across multi-service compositions.
data.product_purpose: Provide a governed GTM agent system that helps revenue and GTM users inspect pipeline and forecast posture, identify risk and bottlenecks, review team and product performance, enrich account context, prioritize work, draft outreach, and prepare operational next steps without exposing raw data or allowing unsafe execution.
data.scope_summary: The product is a multi-service GTM showcase spanning pipeline analytics, enrichment, prioritization, outreach drafting, actor-aware permission boundaries, approval-gated operational previews, auditability, and governed cross-service composition. It is not a single unconstrained GTM super-agent, generic BI query builder, raw CRM export surface, or autonomous execution system.
data.success_outcome_summary: The product succeeds when reviewers can see that business intent was defined first, bounded services answer recurring GTM questions reliably, permissions and approvals remain visible across compound workflows, generated implementations match the intended design, and multiple agent runtimes can consume the same governed services.
data.title: GTM Revenue Operations Showcase

items.title: Canonical capability contracts

questions_for_user: What are the reviewed implementation input names, types, required flags, defaults, and allowed values for each source-owned capability?

Confirm whether these source-declared capability IDs are the canonical contract IDs, and reject any paraphrased duplicates before generation: gtm.pipeline_summary, gtm.pipeline_forecast_summary, gtm.stage_bottleneck_summary, gtm.sales_team_performance_summary, gtm.product_pipeline_summary, gtm.stalled_opportunity_review, gtm.account_risk_summary, gtm.prepare_followup_tasks, gtm.prepare_reassignment_plan, gtm.at_risk_followup_preparation, gtm.at_risk_reassignment_preparation, gtm.account_enrichment_summary, gtm.lookalike_accounts, gtm.at_risk_account_enrichment_summary, gtm.score_leads, gtm.prioritize_accounts, gtm.route_leads, gtm.prioritized_routing_preparation, gtm.draft_outreach_message, gtm.suggest_followup_content
envelope.summary: Compiled reviewed canonical capability inventory into concrete Developer Design capability contracts. Because the source includes machine-readable interface evidence, Studio preserves it deterministically instead of asking the model to retype it.
envelope.title: Capability Formalization Proposal: GTM Pipeline Q2 Review
watchouts: Review side-effect posture, backend operation labels, and output shape names before locking Developer Design if the source inventory did not specify them.
Input names, types, required flags, defaults, allowed values, semantic types, and resolution posture were copied from the reviewed inventory.

next_steps: Review and accept or edit the candidate policy blocks below.
Decide whether actor families are named directly in bindings or referenced through external authority policy.
Confirm whether audit explanation should be modeled as explicit runtime surface or metadata-only requirement.
Use accepted blocks to draft concrete governance bindings per capability and service.

items.body: Define runtime policy so bounded read capabilities in pipeline, enrichment, and prioritization evaluate actor authority before execution and return governed outcomes that preserve allowed scope. When a requested region, owner scope, or visibility boundary exceeds actor authority, the runtime must either narrow to actor-safe scope with explicit restricted behavior or mask protected financial values where partial visibility is allowed. The binding should apply to quarter-scoped pipeline summaries and other bounded reads that accept owner_scope or inherit actor scope by default. The policy must preserve explainable differences between actors and prohibit bypass through prompt interpretation or consumer-side glue.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly requires actor-aware visibility, restricted versus denied behavior, masked financial values, and explainable differences across actors. Input contracts repeatedly use actor_policy_or_explicit with use_actor_scope defaults, and scenarios call out forecast restriction and masking.
items.title: Bind actor-aware scope restriction and masking across bounded read capabilities

items.body: Define runtime policy so capabilities stop with clarification when required bounded inputs are missing, ambiguous, or unresolved, rather than inferring them from date, conversation history, or hidden defaults. This should cover quarter-bound pipeline and operational preview capabilities, explicit account selection for enrichment, reference account for lookalike analysis, cohort references for prioritization, and target/objective context for outreach drafting. The binding should preserve per-input resolution behavior from the completed input contracts, including clarify on missing quarter, clarify on unresolved entity references, and omit only where the contract explicitly allows omission.
items.rationale: The baseline repeatedly requires clarification instead of guessing. Completed input contracts provide concrete on_missing, on_ambiguous, and on_unresolved behavior for quarter, cohort_ref, account references, target_ref, and related inputs.
items.title: Bind clarification-required stops for missing critical bounded inputs

items.body: Define runtime policy so operational preparation capabilities produce preview artifacts and approval_required outcomes only, never execution. This applies to reassignment planning, follow-up preparation, routing preview, and composed preparation flows. The binding should state that preview generation is allowed only within actor authority, that approval tokens may be prepared for later delegation, and that direct execution requests are denied or stopped outside the current surface. The policy must also prohibit hidden writes, ownership changes, queue mutation, or dispatch side effects before explicit approval on a separate execution path.
items.rationale: The baseline requires a hard approval boundary, scoped authority, and delegation tokens. Runtime governance marks prepare_followup_tasks, prepare_reassignment_plan, route_leads, at_risk_followup_preparation, at_risk_reassignment_preparation, and prioritized_routing_preparation as approval_gated with approval_required and preview-only outputs.
items.title: Bind hard approval boundary for operational previews and routing preparation

items.body: Define runtime policy so outreach capabilities produce draft content only and never send messages, mutate engagement state, dispatch externally, or export raw source content. The binding should cover direct outreach drafting, follow-up variants, objection-response variants, and prioritized outreach drafting. It should require explicit target context where the contract requires it, allow bounded channel and objective selection, and deny send requests, transcript dumps, training-corpus exports, or any attempt to turn drafting into outbound execution. For composed outreach flows, the policy must preserve bounded target selection and keep prioritization logic separate from the draft generation step unless the provider-owned public capability explicitly owns that composition.
items.rationale: The baseline says outreach is draft-only and must deny send behavior and raw transcript export. Runtime governance for outreach capabilities explicitly excludes external_dispatch and system.mutation, while scenarios require preserving draft-only behavior across direct and prioritized outreach flows.
items.title: Bind draft-only outreach posture with explicit denial of send and mutation behavior

items.body: Define runtime policy for composed capabilities so child clarification, denial, restriction, and approval_required outcomes propagate predictably to the parent surface while preserving service-owned capability boundaries. Where completed composition evidence exists, the binding should preserve same-service step ordering, mapped inputs, mapped outputs, and fail-parent behavior on child errors. Where runtime evidence marks a public capability as atomic provider-owned, the binding should treat internal derivation as implementation detail and avoid exposing unsupported child-step contracts. Cross-service handoffs must preserve bounded target or cohort scope, actor-aware policy evaluation, and audit lineage across the chain.
items.rationale: The baseline requires governed cross-service composition without hidden app glue. Composition evidence explicitly defines propagation rules for several composed capabilities, while other public capabilities are marked atomic provider-owned. The service shape also preserves service ownership and handoff relationships.
items.title: Bind composed capability outcome propagation without collapsing service ownership

items.body: Define runtime policy so read capabilities return bounded summaries, recommendations, contributors, or evidence only, and explicitly deny raw row exports, raw enrichment dumps, raw model features, training-detail exports, and similar backend internals. The binding should distinguish allowed explainable evidence from prohibited raw data access across pipeline review, enrichment, and prioritization. It should also ensure that limit inputs constrain bounded result sets when provided, without turning the surface into generic data extraction.
items.rationale: The baseline repeatedly prohibits raw CRM exports, raw enrichment exports, and raw model-feature or training-detail exports. Runtime governance rows consistently specify content.summary or content.recommendation outputs and list raw_data_export or raw_model_features in does_not_produce.
items.title: Bind bounded read outputs and explicit denial of raw exports or backend internals

items.body: Define runtime policy so every governed invocation records enough metadata to reconstruct who asked, which actor identity applied, which canonical capability ran, which normalized parameters were used, what governed outcome occurred, and why a different actor could receive a different result. The binding should require durable and searchable audit records, child invocation recording for composed flows where supported, and lineage fields including task_id, invocation_id, parent_invocation_id, and client_reference_id. It should also require that clarification stops, restrictions, masking, denials, successes, and approval_required outcomes are all reviewable after the fact.
items.rationale: Auditability is a locked requirement, not an implementation detail. The baseline explicitly requires reconstructable governed calls and explanation of actor-differentiated outcomes. Requirements and composition evidence also call for parent-child lineage and child invocation recording.
items.title: Bind durable audit lineage and explanation requirements for every governed call

questions_for_user: Should audit explanation of actor-differentiated outcomes be bound as a first-class runtime review capability now, or left as cross-cutting metadata only?
Do you want explicit actor families named in policy bindings now, or should bindings stay actor-agnostic and reference authority/scope rules only?
For approval_required flows, should the binding stop at preview generation only, or also define the expected approval token handoff shape for later execution surfaces?
Should composed capabilities be bound as provider-owned atomic runtime surfaces where evidence says so, or should policy still expose child-step propagation details for review?
envelope.summary: Candidate runtime policy binding blocks derived from the locked GTM baseline and completed developer evidence. Focus is on actor-aware scope, clarification stops, approval boundaries, composition propagation, and audit lineage across the bounded GTM capabilities.
envelope.title: Runtime Policy Binding Proposal: GTM Pipeline Q2 Review
watchouts: The baseline requires stable canonical capability IDs; policy bindings should not rename capabilities or collapse service ownership.

Several composed user scenarios exist, but runtime evidence marks some public capabilities as atomic provider-owned surfaces; do not force child-step exposure where the evidence says composition is internal.
Approval-gated capabilities must stop before execution; bindings must not imply downstream mutation authority.
Owner scope often defaults to actor-safe scope when omitted; bindings must distinguish omitted scope from denied out-of-scope requests.
Clarification, restricted, denied, masked, success, and approval_required are distinct governed outcomes and should not be merged into generic error handling.
Audit durability and searchable reconstruction are required; bindings should preserve task, invocation, parent invocation, and client reference lineage.

next_steps: Confirm the concrete backend systems and resolver sources for each service area.
Decide whether the production single-service preference means one deployable service with internal bounded modules or one public gateway over multiple internal services.
Select the audit store and policy engine binding so governed outcomes are reconstructable.
Review and accept or edit the candidate blocks below before using them in developer design or generation settings.

items.body: Expose a single public HTTP service for gtm-pipeline-q2-review, but preserve backend routing by bounded service area: pipeline, enrichment, prioritization, and outreach. Route each canonical capability to its owning backend module or internal service based on the locked capability inventory. Keep lineage fields on every hop: task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id. Use signed requests and scoped delegation tokens for any internal hop so cross-service composition remains explicit and auditable.
items.rationale: The baseline requires public_http_service, HTTP transport, production_single_service preference, signed trust mode, scoped authority, delegation tokens, and lineage across composed flows.
items.title: Bind the package as one public HTTP governed entrypoint with internal bounded service routing

items.body: Bind read-only summary capabilities to governed read backends that can return aggregate evidence without raw exports. This includes forecast, bottleneck, sales team, product pipeline, account enrichment, lookalikes, lead scoring, account prioritization, and outreach drafting context. Bind approval-gated capabilities to preview-producing backends only: prepare_followup_tasks, prepare_reassignment_plan, route_leads, at_risk_followup_preparation, at_risk_reassignment_preparation, bottleneck_account_outreach_draft, and prioritized_routing_preparation must generate reviewable previews and approval requests but must not execute downstream mutations.
items.rationale: Runtime governance explicitly separates read capabilities from approval_required preview capabilities and forbids raw exports and execution for first-cut operational flows.
items.title: Separate read-model bindings from approval-preview bindings

items.body: Provide backend resolver bindings for the catalogs referenced in the input contracts: account catalog, account-or-lead catalog, product catalog, and cohort catalog. Resolution must support clarify on missing, ambiguous, or unresolved references where specified, and preserve actor-safe scope defaults for owner_scope inputs. Closed-value inputs such as forecast_mode, slice_by, objective, target_queue, objection_theme, ranking_basis, and selection_basis should be normalized in the backend contract layer rather than inferred downstream.
items.rationale: Completed input-contract evidence names concrete resolver and catalog references plus normalization behavior that must be implemented consistently for governed clarification and restriction outcomes.
items.title: Bind entity and cohort resolution to explicit backend catalogs

items.body: Insert a policy enforcement layer ahead of backend reads and preview generation to evaluate actor scope, purpose binding, and restricted-versus-denied outcomes. This layer should narrow owner_scope when actor policy allows partial access, mask financial values where required, and return explicit restricted or denied outcomes instead of overfetching then filtering informally. The same policy decision point should be reused across single-service and composed flows so two actors can receive different governed outcomes with explainable reasons.
items.rationale: The baseline and scenarios require actor-aware visibility, masking, restricted outcomes, and explainability for why different actors received different results.
items.title: Bind actor-aware policy enforcement before backend data access

items.body: Write an audit record for every governed call and child call with actor identity, capability ID, normalized parameters, governed outcome, masking or restriction reason, approval stop if present, and parent-child lineage. For composed capabilities, record child invocations and parent task lineage exactly as indicated by the composition evidence. Make the audit store searchable by actor, capability, quarter, cohort, account reference, invocation_id, and parent_invocation_id so reviewers can reconstruct outcome differences across flows.
items.rationale: Durable searchable audit is a locked requirement, and composition evidence explicitly calls for recording child invocations and parent task lineage.
items.title: Bind every capability invocation to a durable searchable governance audit store

items.body: Implement composed capabilities using explicit backend orchestration owned by the provider service for each public capability. Preserve the reviewed compositions already evidenced: prioritized_routing_preparation composes prioritize_accounts then route_leads; at_risk_followup_preparation composes follow-up preview preparation; at_risk_reassignment_preparation composes reassignment preview preparation. Treat prioritized_outreach_draft and at_risk_account_enrichment_summary as provider-owned composed capabilities at the contract level, but do not expose extra public sub-steps beyond the canonical capability surface.
items.rationale: Composition evidence distinguishes explicit same-service compositions from provider-owned atomic contract surfaces, and the baseline warns against hiding policy in app glue.
items.title: Bind composed capabilities through explicit orchestration contracts, not hidden consumer logic

items.body: Connect outreach capabilities only to draft-generation backends and optional draft persistence surfaces. Do not bind draft_outreach_message, suggest_followup_content, objection_response_variants, prioritized_outreach_draft, or bottleneck_account_outreach_draft to email delivery, sequencing, CRM activity mutation, or transcript export systems. If a downstream engagement platform exists, bind it only as a future approval or handoff target outside the current execution path.
items.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance make outreach draft-only, explicitly excluding external dispatch, system mutation, and raw source export.
items.title: Bind outreach generation to draft storage or return payloads only, never send infrastructure

questions_for_user: Which concrete data systems back the pipeline, enrichment, prioritization, and outreach services today (for example warehouse, semantic layer, CRM mirror, enrichment provider, or model service)?
Should all four services be deployed inside one runtime process with internal module boundaries, or as separately addressable HTTP services behind one public entrypoint?
What system should own entity resolution for account, lead, product, and cohort catalogs referenced by the input contracts?
For approval-gated previews, which downstream system should receive approval requests or preview artifacts, if any, before execution remains blocked?
What signing and delegation-token mechanism should backend calls use between service areas and any downstream systems?
Where should durable searchable audit records live, and is there an existing audit store or observability platform to bind to?
envelope.summary: Candidate backend-binding blocks derived from the locked GTM baseline and completed developer evidence. Focus is on binding the public HTTP service to concrete governed backends, resolvers, and audit surfaces without changing capability boundaries.
envelope.title: Backend binding proposal for GTM Pipeline Q2 Review
watchouts: Do not collapse bounded capabilities into a generic CRM or warehouse query layer; bindings must preserve the canonical capability contract.

Cross-service capabilities such as prioritized routing preparation and prioritized outreach draft are governed compositions; backend bindings should preserve lineage and child invocation audit, not hide orchestration in app glue.
Approval-gated capabilities must stop at preview generation; no binding should directly execute routing, reassignment, follow-up creation, or message send.
Actor-aware restriction and masking need a concrete policy evaluation point in the backend path; do not leave this as prompt-only behavior.
Several inputs rely on backend-resolved catalogs and closed-value normalization; missing resolver bindings will break clarification and normalization behavior.
Audit requirements are first-class product behavior, so backend bindings must capture actor, normalized inputs, capability, outcome, and parent-child lineage IDs.

next_steps: Review and accept the candidate blocks that match the intended verification scope.
Decide whether to organize the final verification artifact by cross-cutting concern, by capability family, or by scenario pack.
Confirm whether named actor examples should be included in the accepted verification expectations.
Use accepted blocks to draft the formal verification expectations artifact and coverage matrix.

items.body: Verification should prove that each canonical capability returns only governed outcomes supported by the baseline: success for in-scope reads or drafts, clarification when critical scope is missing, restriction when actor scope is narrower, denial for unsafe or out-of-scope asks, and approval_required for operational previews. Tests should explicitly confirm that required inputs such as quarter, cohort_ref, account_names, reference_account, or target_ref are not silently inferred when the input contract says clarify on missing.
items.rationale: The locked baseline repeatedly requires explicit governed outcomes and forbids silent guessing. Input-contract evidence also marks many critical fields as clarify-on-missing.
items.title: Verify governed outcomes and required clarification behavior

items.body: Verification should confirm that runtime metadata, regression coverage, and developer-facing evidence all preserve the canonical capability IDs and their declared input contracts without paraphrasing or alias drift. Checks should cover atomic and composed capabilities across pipeline review, enrichment, prioritization, routing preview, and outreach drafting so the same contract surface is visible from design through runtime evidence.
items.rationale: The baseline makes canonical capability preservation a product requirement, and the evidence bundle already provides completed capability inventory, input contracts, runtime governance, and composition metadata.
items.title: Verify preservation of the canonical capability contract

items.body: Verification should include named actor-differentiated cases that show how visibility, masking, allowed scope, and governed outcomes change by actor identity. At minimum, checks should prove that a broader GTM leader can receive allowed forecast or product pipeline summaries while narrower actors are restricted from disallowed scope or receive masked financial values, and that the system can explain why the outcomes differed.
items.rationale: Actor-aware governance is central in the baseline and is directly supported by scenarios for forecast restriction/masking and audit explanation of different outcomes.
items.title: Verify actor-aware restriction, masking, and differential outcomes

items.body: Verification should prove that read-oriented capabilities return bounded summaries, explainable rollups, contributors, or rationale rather than raw row-level exports, raw enrichment dumps, or raw model-feature details. Coverage should include forecast, stage bottlenecks, sales team performance, product pipeline, account enrichment, lookalike analysis, and prioritization outputs, with explicit denial or exclusion checks for unsupported export-style requests.
items.rationale: Runtime governance evidence explicitly marks these capabilities as summary or recommendation outputs and explicitly excludes raw_data_export or raw_model_features.
items.title: Verify bounded read contracts and non-export guardrails

items.body: Verification should prove that reassignment planning, follow-up preparation, routing preview, and other operational-preview capabilities stop at approval_required and do not execute downstream mutations. Checks should confirm that preview content is returned, approval execution is not produced by these calls, and direct execution asks are denied or stopped rather than carried out.
items.rationale: The baseline requires a hard separation between preparation and execution, and runtime governance marks these capabilities as approval_gated with approval_required outputs and no execution side effects.
items.title: Verify hard approval boundaries for operational previews

items.body: Verification should cover composed flows such as prioritized target to outreach draft, at-risk pipeline review to enrichment, and prioritization to routing preview. For each composed flow, checks should confirm that bounded scope is preserved across steps, child clarification or denial propagates correctly, lineage is recorded across parent and child invocations, and the flow does not blur service-owned responsibilities into a generic query-anything surface.
items.rationale: The baseline explicitly requires governed cross-service composition, and the composition evidence includes propagation policy and parent-child audit lineage for composed capabilities.
items.title: Verify cross-service composition preserves scope, lineage, and service boundaries

items.body: Verification should prove that outreach capabilities produce draft content only, require an explicit target or bounded prioritized cohort as defined by contract, support bounded variants when requested, and deny send behavior, transcript dumps, or backend mutation. Checks should distinguish direct draft generation from composed prioritized outreach so prioritization logic does not leak into simple target-based drafting and send behavior never appears in either path.
items.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance both define outreach as draft-only, with explicit denial of send-like behavior and separate contracts for direct and composed outreach flows.
items.title: Verify outreach remains draft-only and target-bounded

items.body: Verification should require evidence that a reviewer can reconstruct who asked, which actor identity applied, which capability ran, which normalized parameters were used, what governed outcome occurred, and why another actor may have received a different result. This should be validated across success, clarification, restriction, denial, masking, and approval_required outcomes, not only happy-path success cases.
items.rationale: Auditability is a first-class business requirement in the baseline and is reinforced by the dedicated scenario about explaining different governed outcomes after the fact.
items.title: Verify post-hoc audit reconstruction for single-service and composed calls

questions_for_user: Do you want verification expectations to cover all 23 canonical capabilities now, or only the highest-risk flows first?
Should audit explanation be verified as a standalone cross-cutting requirement, or only through per-capability checks?
Do you want explicit regression expectations for both atomic capabilities and composed capabilities in this pass?
Should actor-differentiated outcomes be verified against named actor examples, or only against generic policy classes?
envelope.summary: Candidate developer-facing verification expectation blocks derived from the locked GTM baseline, scenario set, and completed runtime evidence. Focus is on governed outcomes, canonical capability contracts, actor-aware behavior, approval boundaries, and auditability.
envelope.title: Verification Expectations Proposal: GTM Pipeline Q2 Review
watchouts: Do not let verification rewrite the locked capability names or collapse service-owned boundaries into generic GTM behaviors.
Cross-service scenarios in the baseline should not be verified as hidden app glue; lineage and governed outcomes must remain visible across steps.
Approval-gated previews must be verified as stopping before execution, even when internal composition includes routing or reassignment preparation steps.
Input-contract defaults should not be mistaken for permission to infer missing required scope such as quarter, cohort, account set, or target.
Actor-aware restriction and masking need explicit verification evidence, not just successful happy-path responses.

services.name: Gtm Outreach
responsibilities: Own a reviewed portion of the bounded workflow, policy checks, clarification stops, and governed response contract.

items.title: Draft a concrete service shape from the available product intent

items.body: Define the stable capability contracts each service is expected to own, including which actions are read-only, which require approval, and which must stop for clarification.
items.rationale: The generator and verifier need stable capability ownership, not only narrative service descriptions.
items.title: Formalize the capability surface each service must expose

items.body: Capture where one service depends on another for bounded context, approvals, or downstream execution so coordination can be represented explicitly instead of as hidden glue.
items.rationale: Coordination edges are easier to validate when they are declared before implementation.
items.title: Describe the required coordination edges between services

items.body: Identify which service should prepare a proposed action and which surface or role must approve it before the mutation boundary is crossed.
items.rationale: Approval semantics should be explicit in service design rather than buried in one implementation path.
items.title: Keep approval boundaries outside the main happy path

items.body: Define which service boundaries must preserve explanation, lineage, or durable evidence so later verification can reconstruct why a result or stop condition occurred.
items.rationale: Cross-service traceability should be deliberate, not inferred after implementation exists.
items.title: Preserve explanation and traceability across service boundaries

items.title: Canonical capability contracts

pm_artifacts.title: Capability Formalization Proposal: GTM Pipeline Q2 Review

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy so bounded read capabilities in pipeline, enrichment, and prioritization evaluate actor authority before execution and return governed outcomes that preserve allowed scope. When a requested region, owner scope, or visibility boundary exceeds actor authority, the runtime must either narrow to actor-safe scope with explicit restricted behavior or mask protected financial values where partial visibility is allowed. The binding should apply to quarter-scoped pipeline summaries and other bounded reads that accept owner_scope or inherit actor scope by default. The policy must preserve explainable differences between actors and prohibit bypass through prompt interpretation or consumer-side glue.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline explicitly requires actor-aware visibility, restricted versus denied behavior, masked financial values, and explainable differences across actors. Input contracts repeatedly use actor_policy_or_explicit with use_actor_scope defaults, and scenarios call out forecast restriction and masking.
accepted_payload.title: Bind actor-aware scope restriction and masking across bounded read capabilities

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy so capabilities stop with clarification when required bounded inputs are missing, ambiguous, or unresolved, rather than inferring them from date, conversation history, or hidden defaults. This should cover quarter-bound pipeline and operational preview capabilities, explicit account selection for enrichment, reference account for lookalike analysis, cohort references for prioritization, and target/objective context for outreach drafting. The binding should preserve per-input resolution behavior from the completed input contracts, including clarify on missing quarter, clarify on unresolved entity references, and omit only where the contract explicitly allows omission.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline repeatedly requires clarification instead of guessing. Completed input contracts provide concrete on_missing, on_ambiguous, and on_unresolved behavior for quarter, cohort_ref, account references, target_ref, and related inputs.
accepted_payload.title: Bind clarification-required stops for missing critical bounded inputs

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy so operational preparation capabilities produce preview artifacts and approval_required outcomes only, never execution. This applies to reassignment planning, follow-up preparation, routing preview, and composed preparation flows. The binding should state that preview generation is allowed only within actor authority, that approval tokens may be prepared for later delegation, and that direct execution requests are denied or stopped outside the current surface. The policy must also prohibit hidden writes, ownership changes, queue mutation, or dispatch side effects before explicit approval on a separate execution path.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline requires a hard approval boundary, scoped authority, and delegation tokens. Runtime governance marks prepare_followup_tasks, prepare_reassignment_plan, route_leads, at_risk_followup_preparation, at_risk_reassignment_preparation, and prioritized_routing_preparation as approval_gated with approval_required and preview-only outputs.
accepted_payload.title: Bind hard approval boundary for operational previews and routing preparation

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy so outreach capabilities produce draft content only and never send messages, mutate engagement state, dispatch externally, or export raw source content. The binding should cover direct outreach drafting, follow-up variants, objection-response variants, and prioritized outreach drafting. It should require explicit target context where the contract requires it, allow bounded channel and objective selection, and deny send requests, transcript dumps, training-corpus exports, or any attempt to turn drafting into outbound execution. For composed outreach flows, the policy must preserve bounded target selection and keep prioritization logic separate from the draft generation step unless the provider-owned public capability explicitly owns that composition.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline says outreach is draft-only and must deny send behavior and raw transcript export. Runtime governance for outreach capabilities explicitly excludes external_dispatch and system.mutation, while scenarios require preserving draft-only behavior across direct and prioritized outreach flows.
accepted_payload.title: Bind draft-only outreach posture with explicit denial of send and mutation behavior

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy for composed capabilities so child clarification, denial, restriction, and approval_required outcomes propagate predictably to the parent surface while preserving service-owned capability boundaries. Where completed composition evidence exists, the binding should preserve same-service step ordering, mapped inputs, mapped outputs, and fail-parent behavior on child errors. Where runtime evidence marks a public capability as atomic provider-owned, the binding should treat internal derivation as implementation detail and avoid exposing unsupported child-step contracts. Cross-service handoffs must preserve bounded target or cohort scope, actor-aware policy evaluation, and audit lineage across the chain.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline requires governed cross-service composition without hidden app glue. Composition evidence explicitly defines propagation rules for several composed capabilities, while other public capabilities are marked atomic provider-owned. The service shape also preserves service ownership and handoff relationships.
accepted_payload.title: Bind composed capability outcome propagation without collapsing service ownership

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy so read capabilities return bounded summaries, recommendations, contributors, or evidence only, and explicitly deny raw row exports, raw enrichment dumps, raw model features, training-detail exports, and similar backend internals. The binding should distinguish allowed explainable evidence from prohibited raw data access across pipeline review, enrichment, and prioritization. It should also ensure that limit inputs constrain bounded result sets when provided, without turning the surface into generic data extraction.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline repeatedly prohibits raw CRM exports, raw enrichment exports, and raw model-feature or training-detail exports. Runtime governance rows consistently specify content.summary or content.recommendation outputs and list raw_data_export or raw_model_features in does_not_produce.
accepted_payload.title: Bind bounded read outputs and explicit denial of raw exports or backend internals

accepted_payload.body: Define runtime policy so every governed invocation records enough metadata to reconstruct who asked, which actor identity applied, which canonical capability ran, which normalized parameters were used, what governed outcome occurred, and why a different actor could receive a different result. The binding should require durable and searchable audit records, child invocation recording for composed flows where supported, and lineage fields including task_id, invocation_id, parent_invocation_id, and client_reference_id. It should also require that clarification stops, restrictions, masking, denials, successes, and approval_required outcomes are all reviewable after the fact.
accepted_payload.rationale: Auditability is a locked requirement, not an implementation detail. The baseline explicitly requires reconstructable governed calls and explanation of actor-differentiated outcomes. Requirements and composition evidence also call for parent-child lineage and child invocation recording.
accepted_payload.title: Bind durable audit lineage and explanation requirements for every governed call

pm_artifacts.title: Runtime Policy Binding Proposal: GTM Pipeline Q2 Review

accepted_payload.body: Expose a single public HTTP service for gtm-pipeline-q2-review, but preserve backend routing by bounded service area: pipeline, enrichment, prioritization, and outreach. Route each canonical capability to its owning backend module or internal service based on the locked capability inventory. Keep lineage fields on every hop: task_id, invocation_id, client_reference_id, and parent_invocation_id. Use signed requests and scoped delegation tokens for any internal hop so cross-service composition remains explicit and auditable.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline requires public_http_service, HTTP transport, production_single_service preference, signed trust mode, scoped authority, delegation tokens, and lineage across composed flows.
accepted_payload.title: Bind the package as one public HTTP governed entrypoint with internal bounded service routing

accepted_payload.body: Bind read-only summary capabilities to governed read backends that can return aggregate evidence without raw exports. This includes forecast, bottleneck, sales team, product pipeline, account enrichment, lookalikes, lead scoring, account prioritization, and outreach drafting context. Bind approval-gated capabilities to preview-producing backends only: prepare_followup_tasks, prepare_reassignment_plan, route_leads, at_risk_followup_preparation, at_risk_reassignment_preparation, bottleneck_account_outreach_draft, and prioritized_routing_preparation must generate reviewable previews and approval requests but must not execute downstream mutations.
accepted_payload.rationale: Runtime governance explicitly separates read capabilities from approval_required preview capabilities and forbids raw exports and execution for first-cut operational flows.
accepted_payload.title: Separate read-model bindings from approval-preview bindings

accepted_payload.body: Provide backend resolver bindings for the catalogs referenced in the input contracts: account catalog, account-or-lead catalog, product catalog, and cohort catalog. Resolution must support clarify on missing, ambiguous, or unresolved references where specified, and preserve actor-safe scope defaults for owner_scope inputs. Closed-value inputs such as forecast_mode, slice_by, objective, target_queue, objection_theme, ranking_basis, and selection_basis should be normalized in the backend contract layer rather than inferred downstream.
accepted_payload.rationale: Completed input-contract evidence names concrete resolver and catalog references plus normalization behavior that must be implemented consistently for governed clarification and restriction outcomes.
accepted_payload.title: Bind entity and cohort resolution to explicit backend catalogs

accepted_payload.body: Insert a policy enforcement layer ahead of backend reads and preview generation to evaluate actor scope, purpose binding, and restricted-versus-denied outcomes. This layer should narrow owner_scope when actor policy allows partial access, mask financial values where required, and return explicit restricted or denied outcomes instead of overfetching then filtering informally. The same policy decision point should be reused across single-service and composed flows so two actors can receive different governed outcomes with explainable reasons.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and scenarios require actor-aware visibility, masking, restricted outcomes, and explainability for why different actors received different results.
accepted_payload.title: Bind actor-aware policy enforcement before backend data access

accepted_payload.body: Write an audit record for every governed call and child call with actor identity, capability ID, normalized parameters, governed outcome, masking or restriction reason, approval stop if present, and parent-child lineage. For composed capabilities, record child invocations and parent task lineage exactly as indicated by the composition evidence. Make the audit store searchable by actor, capability, quarter, cohort, account reference, invocation_id, and parent_invocation_id so reviewers can reconstruct outcome differences across flows.
accepted_payload.rationale: Durable searchable audit is a locked requirement, and composition evidence explicitly calls for recording child invocations and parent task lineage.
accepted_payload.title: Bind every capability invocation to a durable searchable governance audit store

accepted_payload.body: Implement composed capabilities using explicit backend orchestration owned by the provider service for each public capability. Preserve the reviewed compositions already evidenced: prioritized_routing_preparation composes prioritize_accounts then route_leads; at_risk_followup_preparation composes follow-up preview preparation; at_risk_reassignment_preparation composes reassignment preview preparation. Treat prioritized_outreach_draft and at_risk_account_enrichment_summary as provider-owned composed capabilities at the contract level, but do not expose extra public sub-steps beyond the canonical capability surface.
accepted_payload.rationale: Composition evidence distinguishes explicit same-service compositions from provider-owned atomic contract surfaces, and the baseline warns against hiding policy in app glue.
accepted_payload.title: Bind composed capabilities through explicit orchestration contracts, not hidden consumer logic

accepted_payload.body: Connect outreach capabilities only to draft-generation backends and optional draft persistence surfaces. Do not bind draft_outreach_message, suggest_followup_content, objection_response_variants, prioritized_outreach_draft, or bottleneck_account_outreach_draft to email delivery, sequencing, CRM activity mutation, or transcript export systems. If a downstream engagement platform exists, bind it only as a future approval or handoff target outside the current execution path.
accepted_payload.rationale: The baseline and runtime governance make outreach draft-only, explicitly excluding external dispatch, system mutation, and raw source export.
accepted_payload.title: Bind outreach generation to draft storage or return payloads only, never send infrastructure
